title: Exodus 39
subtitle: Ephod and Breastpiece Made; Remaining Garments; Inspection and Blessing

sections.header: Verses 1—21 — The Priestly Garments: Ephod, Breastpiece, Robe

heb.paragraph: V.1,5,7,21,26,29,31,43: The phrase "as the LORD commanded Moses" (kĕʾăsher tsiwwāh ʾet-mōsheh) appears seven times in Exodus 39 as the Tabernacle garments are completed and inspected. The sevenfold repetition echoes the seven days of creation (Gen 1) — the completion of the Tabernacle is a new creation, ordered and blessed just as the original. When Moses inspected the work and saw that "they had done it just as the LORD commanded" (39:43), he "blessed them" — as God had blessed the creation.

V.1,3,5,29: The scarlet thread (shĕnî tôlāʿat — literally "crimson of the worm") woven into the priestly garments is the dye extracted from the cochineal insect (kermes or coccus ilicis). It appears alongside blue and purple in all the sacred textiles. Scarlet appears also in the Passover Rahab narrative (Josh 2:18), in David's lament (2 Sam 1:24), and in Prov 31:21. Isaiah 1:18: "Though your sins are like scarlet (kĕshānî), they shall be white as snow."

panels.hist: The priestly garment construction follows the instructions of chapter 28 with precise fidelity. The twelve gemstones on the breastpiece — carnelian, chrysolite, beryl, turquoise, lapis lazuli, emerald, jacinth, agate, amethyst, topaz, onyx, and jasper — represent the finest stones available through Late Bronze Age trade networks. Several identifications remain uncertain; the Hebrew terms may not correspond exactly to modern gemological categories. Engraving tribal names on gemstones required lapidary skill (ḥārāš ʾeben) attested in Egyptian workshops.
panels.ctx: The ephod and breastpiece (vv.2–21) are described in the same detail as their instructions (Exod 28:6–30). The twelve stones on the breastpiece — one for each tribe, each engraved with a name — represent the entire community carried over the high priest's heart. The shoulder stones carry the names as a "memorial" (v.7) — a reminder before God. Every tribe, every person, is carried into God's presence by the one who enters on their behalf.

cross.note: Christ "always lives to intercede" — the permanent fulfilment of the high priest's representation of Israel.
Jesus' high-priestly prayer: "I pray for them... I pray also for those who will believe." He carries names over his heart before the Father.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes Moses' inspection and blessing parallel the creation narrative's conclusion (Gen 1:31: 'God saw all that he had made, and it was very good'). The tabernacle is a new creation — a new ordered world where God and humanity can coexist.

Moses' inspection and blessing form the execution section's climax. Alter notes the parallel with Gen 1 is unmistakable — the same inspection-and-blessing sequence that ends the creation account ends the tabernacle construction.
Calvin: 'It was not enough to have done the work accurately, unless Moses added his approval and blessing. In this way God ratified and confirmed all the preparations for his worship.'

The NET note on 'Moses blessed them' explains blessing by the covenant mediator is the authorised conveyance of God's approval and favour — the transmission of the divine benediction through the appointed channel.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moses' inspection and blessing form the execution section's climax. Alter notes the parallel with Gen 1 is unmistakable — the same inspection-and-blessing sequence that ends the creation account ends the tabernacle construction.
Calvin: 'It was not enough to have done the work accurately, unless Moses added his approval and blessing. In this way God ratified and confirmed all the preparations for his worship.'

The NET note on 'Moses blessed them' explains blessing by the covenant mediator is the authorised conveyance of God's approval and favour — the transmission of the divine benediction through the appointed channel.
Calvin: 'It was not enough to have done the work accurately, unless Moses added his approval and blessing. In this way God ratified and confirmed all the preparations for his worship.'

The NET note on 'Moses blessed them' explains blessing by the covenant mediator is the authorised conveyance of God's approval and favour — the transmission of the divine benediction through the appointed channel.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'Moses blessed them' explains blessing by the covenant mediator is the authorised conveyance of God's approval and favour — the transmission of the divine benediction through the appointed channel.

The NET note on Moses blessing the craftsmen at the completion of their work traces the parallel to Gen 1:28 and 2:3 (God's blessing at creation's completion) and to Num 6:22-27 (the Aaronic blessing). The construction of the tabernacle is a creation event — completed work blessed by the covenant mediator, as the original creation was blessed by the divine creator.

MacArthur: As the LORD commanded Moses -- appearing seven times in ch.39 -- is the construction narrative's theological drumbeat: perfect correspondence to divine specification is the standard of faithful worship.

MacArthur: They made the plate, the sacred emblem, out of pure gold and engraved on it: holy to the LORD. The engraved plate is the tabernacle's mission statement in miniature: what the whole sanctuary is designed to produce.

sections.header: Verses 22—43 — Other Garments; Moses Inspects and Blesses the Work

heb.paragraph: V.43 (context for completion): "Moses inspected the work and saw that they had done it just as the LORD commanded. So Moses blessed them." The word bārak (to bless) connects Tabernacle-completion to creation-completion: Gen 1:28 (God blessed the created humans), Gen 2:3 (God blessed the seventh day). Moses acts as God's agent of blessing over the work his people have completed. Sacred construction, done in obedience, deserves the same divine approbation as the original creation.

V.8-21: The breastpiece of judgment (ḥōshen mishpāṭ) bore twelve stones — one for each tribe, engraved with their names. The High Priest carried "the names of the sons of Israel over his heart... as a continuing memorial before the LORD." The people's names were physically present before God whenever the High Priest entered the Most Holy Place. No Israelite was forgotten or anonymous before God: each name was carried by the one who stood for them all.

panels.hist: Moses' inspection and blessing of the completed work (v. 43) parallels the divine inspection in Genesis 1:31 ('God saw all that he had made, and it was very good'). The verbal echo is deliberate — the tabernacle is a microcosm, a re-creation of sacred space. The sevenfold repetition of 'as the LORD commanded Moses' throughout chapters 39–40 mirrors the seven days of creation. Ancient Near Eastern temple-building accounts regularly conclude with a divine or royal inspection and pronouncement of approval.
panels.ctx: The inspection and blessing of v.43 is the theological climax of the construction narrative. The phrase "just as the Lord had commanded Moses" appears seven times in vv.32–43 — one for each day of creation, one for each dimension of the Tabernacle project. The sevenfold confirmation of obedience is the construction narrative's doxology: Israel did everything God said. After the golden calf — where they did what God did not say — this sevenfold confirmation is profound.

cross.note: "God saw all that he had made, and it was very good." Moses' inspection (v.43) deliberately echoes God's creative evaluation. The Tabernacle's construction is a new creation event.
"Well done, good and faithful servant!" Moses' blessing is the OT version of this — the master inspects the servants' work and pronounces blessing for faithful execution.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The breastpiece construction in vv.8–21 receives the most detailed description of any single garment element. Sarna notes the twelve stones, each engraved with a tribal name, are described with the same precision the divine instructions used — confirming complete compliance.

The sevenfold repetition of 'just as the LORD commanded Moses' in ch.39 is the chapter's theological signature. Sarna notes the number seven creates a creation-week parallel: the tabernacle's completion with perfect obedience mirrors the original creation's completion with divine approval. The new covenant sanctuary is a new creation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The breastpiece description mirrors ch.28's instruction with exact verbal correspondence. Alter notes the repetition enacts the theology: the community does what God commanded, word for word. The text's fidelity to its own prior language is the literary form of covenant faithfulness.

The construction completion summary is structured as a formal inventory presentation: Moses inspects and finds everything done exactly as commanded, then blesses the workmen. Alter notes this inspection-and-blessing sequence exactly mirrors God's inspection-and-blessing of creation (Gen 1:31 and 2:3). The tabernacle is Genesis's creation redone in portable covenant form.

Calvin: 'Moses gives his approval to the workmen, that we may know their work was not rashly constructed according to their own mind, but carefully and exactly performed, as they had been commanded.' The inspection validates the obedience; the blessing consecrates it.

Calvin: The repeated 'just as the LORD commanded' is the craftsmen's greatest commendation — not that they were creative or innovative but that they were obedient to the divine specification. In covenant work, faithfulness to what was commanded is the highest artistic achievement.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'just as the LORD commanded Moses' (repeated seven times in ch.39) identifies the sevenfold repetition as creating a creation-week parallel. The sevenfold completion signals that the covenant worship system is a new creation — ordered sacred space established on the model of the original creation week.

The NET note on the sevenfold 'just as the LORD commanded Moses' traces the creation-week parallel. The note identifies seven specific completion notices corresponding to seven days, establishing the tabernacle's construction as a liturgical new creation — ordered sacred space built on the model of the original creation week.

MacArthur: Moses inspected the work and saw that they had done it just as the LORD had commanded. So Moses blessed them. The inspection and blessing mirror God's inspection and blessing at creation (Gen 1:31). The tabernacle is the new creation.

MacArthur: The seven-fold refrain just as the LORD commanded Moses is the construction account's theological signature. Complete obedience to divine specification is the craftsmen's highest achievement. The tabernacle is not an artistic expression but a covenant correspondence — every detail faithful to the pattern shown to Moses on the mountain.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: LORD is a key figure in Scripture Walkthrough.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: The sevenfold confirmation of obedience is the construction narrative's doxology: Israel did everything God said…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The people of Israel had done all the work just as the Lord had commanded Moses. And Moses saw all the work, and behold, they had done it; as the Lord had commanded, so had they done it. Then Moses blessed them."NIV"The Israelites had done all the work just as the Lord had commanded Moses. Moses inspected the work and saw that they had done it just as the Lord had commanded. So Moses blessed them."

src.title: Seven-fold Obedience
src.quote: The phrase "as the Lord commanded Moses" appears seven times in Exod 39:1–43 (vv.1,5,7,21,26,29,31,42,43 — depending on the text). The sevenfold repetition mirrors the seven days of creation, presenting the Tabernacle's completion as a new creation.
src.note: The literary structure of Exod 39 deliberately echoes Gen 1.

src.title: Gold Plate Inscription
src.quote: Archaeological finds of inscribed gold and silver objects from the ancient Near East confirm the practice of engraved dedications. The "Holy to the Lord" inscription on the high priest's forehead may be unique in its use as personal adornment rather than architectural dedication.
src.note: The inscription's use is distinctive even within the ANE epigraphic tradition.

rec.text: The deliberate echo: "God saw all that he had made... it was very good" → "Moses saw all the work... they had done it." The Tabernacle's completion is a new-creation moment.
Paul's principle of working for the Lord echoes the Tabernacle's "just as the Lord commanded" — all work done for God is done with the same standard: exactly as he directs.

rows.label: Seven Times: The New Creation Signature
rows.text: Just as the LORD commanded Moses -- the phrase appears seven times in the completion account. The tabernacle's construction with perfect obedience mirrors the original creation's completion with divine approval. The new covenant sanctuary is a new creation.

rows.label: Moses Blesses the Craftsmen
rows.text: When Moses saw that they had done all the work just as the LORD had commanded, Moses blessed them. The completion inspection and blessing mirror God's inspection and blessing of creation (Gen 1:31 and 2:3). The tabernacle is Genesis's creation redone in portable covenant form.

tx.title: Priestly garments construction — LXX differences
tx.content: MT repeats the garment specifications from chapter 28 in the construction account. The LXX, consistent with its abbreviation of chapters 35–40, reduces the repetition significantly. Where MT says 'as the LORD commanded Moses' seven times (echoing creation's seven days), the LXX count varies by manuscript.
tx.note: The sevenfold 'as the LORD commanded Moses' formula in MT deliberately parallels the seven-day creation account in Genesis 1, equating tabernacle construction with cosmic creation. Some LXX manuscripts preserve all seven.

debate.title: 'Just as the LORD commanded': How Many Times in Ch.39?

positions.name: Seven times specifically in ch.39
positions.argument: The phrase appears seven times in ch.39 (vv.1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) — the creation number. The sevenfold completion of the priestly garments mirrors the seven days of creation.

positions.name: Approximately sixteen times in chs.39–40
positions.argument: Counting all instances in the execution section, the phrase appears approximately sixteen times. The cumulative effect confirms total obedience.
